Name: hardware_aware_neural_network_optimization
Title: Efficient Neural Network Inference on Commodity GPUs via Hardware-Aware Optimization
Short Hypothesis: By optimizing neural networks with hardware-aware techniques, we can achieve significant improvements in inference efficiency on commodity GPUs without sacrificing accuracy. We hypothesize that a combination of quantization, sparsity, and knowledge distillation can be used to optimize neural networks for efficient inference on GPUs.
Related Work: Recent works such as APNAS, HLQ, and SNIP have explored hardware-aware neural network optimization techniques. However, these works focus on specific aspects of optimization, such as quantization or sparsity, and do not provide a comprehensive framework for optimizing neural networks for efficient inference on GPUs. Our proposal builds upon these works and aims to provide a more comprehensive approach to hardware-aware neural network optimization.
Abstract: Deep neural networks have achieved state-of-the-art performance in various applications, but their high computational and memory requirements limit their deployment on edge devices. To address this challenge, we propose a hardware-aware neural network optimization framework that combines quantization, sparsity, and knowledge distillation to optimize neural networks for efficient inference on commodity GPUs. Our framework uses a combination of analytical models and empirical search to find the optimal configuration of quantization, sparsity, and knowledge distillation for a given neural network and GPU architecture. We evaluate our framework on various neural networks and GPU architectures and demonstrate significant improvements in inference efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.
Experiments: We will evaluate our framework on various neural networks, including ResNet, MobileNet, and DenseNet, and GPU architectures, including NVIDIA GeForce and AMD Radeon. We will use metrics such as inference time, memory usage, and accuracy to evaluate the performance of our framework. We will also compare our framework with state-of-the-art optimization techniques, such as APNAS and SNIP, to demonstrate its effectiveness.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is the complexity of the optimization framework, which may require significant computational resources and expertise to implement. Another limitation is the potential trade-off between inference efficiency and accuracy, which may require careful tuning of the optimization parameters. Additionally, the framework may not be applicable to all types of neural networks and GPU architectures, which may require additional modifications and extensions.
